channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Tracking Trump’s Efforts to Reshape Cultural Institutions
item.description: Jennifer Schuessler, a culture reporter who writes about intellectual life, is now covering President Trump’s attempts to amend the presentation of American history.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of teachers posting signs on a former exhibit about slavery at the Independence National Historical Park in Philadelphia.

item.title: Theater Tickets Are Cheaper in London Than New York. What Gives?
item.description: For half the price of a great seat at a Broadway show, you can see “Paddington” in the West End (if you can find a ticket) and snack on a marmalade sandwich.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: An Invisible Bottleneck: A Helium Shortage Threatens the Chip Industry
item.description: With a third of the global supply offline because of the war in Iran, gas companies are scrambling to assure critical A.I. chip makers there will be no disruptions.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Xinyun Wu
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: Air Liquide supplies helium and other gases to the semiconductor industry.

item.title: Stocks Stabilize but Oil Prices Stay High After Trump Delays Bombing Threat
item.description: President Trump said on Thursday he would delay an imminent deadline to begin attacking Iran’s power grid.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Athit Perawongmetha/Reuters

item.title: Trump’s Signature Is Set to Be Added to America’s Currency
item.description: President Trump is poised to be the first sitting president to have his signature appear on the U.S. dollar.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The addition of President Trump’s signature to dollars is the latest example of his emblazoning national institutions with his personal brand.

item.title: Iran Keeps a Tight Grip on Strait of Hormuz, Pressuring Shipping and Energy Sectors
item.description: Iran has allowed a small number of vessels to pass, but that won’t alleviate pressure or risk for the shipping industry and energy markets any time soon.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Ships in the Persian Gulf last week, heading toward the Strait of Hormuz. Iran’s de facto closure of the strait has given the country powerful leverage against the United States and Israel.

item.title: Safety Experts Considered LaGuardia Challenging but Not an Outlier
item.description: Regulators, pilots and others in aviation have worried about the kind of runway accident that happened at LaGuardia Airport on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi and Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Federal Aviation Administration has been working for years to reduce accidents like the one involving an Air Canada plane and a fire truck at LaGuardia Airport in New York City on Sunday.

item.title: At Non-TSA Airports, Wait Times Are Minutes, Not Hours
item.description: A handful of airports, including San Francisco and Kansas City International, participate in a program that lets them rely on private screeners instead of T.S.A. agents.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Average security waits at San Francisco International Airport over the last 30 days have averaged under 10 minutes.

item.title: Oscars Will Move to a Venue Outside Hollywood
item.description: The event will be held in downtown Los Angeles starting in 2029 as part of a deal between the academy and AEG, a developer of entertainment venues.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Walter Cicchetti/Alamy
item.media:description: The Oscars will be held at the Peacock Theater in downtown Los Angeles starting in 2029.

item.title: Mortgage Rates Jump Again as Iran War Effects Ripple Through Housing Market
item.description: The average rate on a 30-year mortgage in the United States hit 6.38 percent, the fourth increase since the war began.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp (Freddie Mac)

item.title: After Wooing Trump With Deals, Pakistan Gets a Seat at the Table
item.description: Steve Witkoff, a diplomatic envoy, used the Board of Peace to announce an agreement that could raze a Pakistan-owned Manhattan hotel. Now the country is involved in negotiating peace talks with Iran.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany, Ben Protess and Elian Peltier
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ahmed Gaber for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Roosevelt Hotel in New York, which is owned by Pakistan, in 2023.

item.title: The Clues Binance Missed That Led to Billions in Crypto Flowing to Iran
item.description: The world’s largest crypto exchange is under fire after investigators found accounts moving $1.7 billion to entities linked to Iran. Clues about those accounts were in plain sight for over a year.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany and Michael Forsythe
item.media:credit: Ore Huiying for The New York Times
item.media:description: A villa in Singapore whose address was shared by the chief executive of Blessed Trust, which was a vendor to Binance, and a Chinese entrepreneur with a corporate office that was on a U.S. blacklist.

item.title: Local Opposition Is Slowing A.I. Data Centers. Wall Street Has Noticed.
item.description: Tech companies are running into resistance from neighbors and may not be able to build at the pace they promised investors.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction of a Meta data center in Eagle Mountain, Utah, in 2024. The company is among those that have staked their futures on the use of artificial intelligence.

item.title: U.S. Accuses NewYork-Presbyterian of Reaching Deals That Kept Costs High
item.description: The Justice Department sued the large New York health system, saying it restricted insurance companies from offering patients cheaper options.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times
item.media:description: The government said that the NewYork-Presbyterian system had kept health plans from steering patients to less expensive options or creating cheaper plans.

item.title: Europe Heads for Another Energy Shock as Iran War Raises Natural Gas Prices
item.description: The region’s stores of natural gas are running at the lowest level in years, and filling them up is increasingly daunting as the U.S.-led war in Iran pushes up prices.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: How to Turn a Chicken Egg Into a Drug Factory
item.description: A biotech start-up is testing a novel way of efficiently producing pharmaceutical drugs.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: War in Iran Will Push U.S. Inflation Above 4 Percent, O.E.C.D. Forecast Says
item.description: Higher energy prices and uncertainty over the war in the Middle East will boost inflation and weigh on economic growth, a new forecast says.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: U.S. inflation is expected to average 4.2 percent this year, more than 1 percentage point higher than the O.E.C.D.’s previous forecast.

item.title: Shield AI, a Start-Up Making Military Drones, Raises $2 Billion
item.description: The company, which develops autonomous military technology, also plans to buy a maker of simulation software as interest in next-generation defense soars.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Caroline Chia/Reuters
item.media:description: Shield AI’s software and drones are being used by several governments, including Ukraine’s.

item.title: Is Big Tech Facing a Big Tobacco Moment?
item.description: Back-to-back courtroom losses have put technology giants, including Meta and Google, in uncertain territory as they face lawsuits and bans on teen users.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The legal heat is growing on social media giants like Meta, and its C.E.O., Mark Zuckerberg.

item.title: Snapchat Investigated in Europe Over Child Safety Policies
item.description: Regulators in Brussels accused the social media platform of maintaining a weak age-verification system, and steering younger users toward inappropriate experiences.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano
item.media:credit: Olivier Matthys/EPA, via Shutterstock
item.media:description: Henna Virkkunen, the European commissioner overseeing digital policy, said that the European Union’s Digital Services Act “demands high safety standards for all users.”

item.title: Help! Budget Charged Me Almost $600 for Returning a Car Early.
item.description: An American who rented a vehicle for a two-week European vacation brought it back to the airport just a few hours ahead of schedule. Why did the price nearly double?
item.dc:creator: Seth Kugel
item.media:credit: Matt Blease

item.title: A Film Studio Moved to Montana for Tax Breaks. Will It Benefit the Locals?
item.description: Story House arrived in Missoula last year with big plans for a movie and television campus that would create hundreds of jobs and build a filmmaking ecosystem.
item.dc:creator: Brian Josephs
item.media:credit: Tailyr Irvine for The New York Times
item.media:description: The founders of Story House moved their studio project to Missoula, Mont., from Wyoming, to take advantage of Montana’s tax credits for film and television production.

item.title: U.S. Stocks Have Their Biggest Drop Since Start of Iran War
item.description: The S&P 500 tumbled 1.7 percent on Thursday and oil prices continued rising as investors fret about when the war would end.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Trump Draws Bipartisan Backlash for Easing Oil Sanctions on Russia and Iran
item.description: Republicans and Democrats alike have criticized the Trump administration’s moves, taken to stabilize oil markets rocked by the war with Iran, warning that it is benefiting two U.S. adversaries.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Amit Dave/Reuters
item.media:description: A tanker carrying Russian oil in Gujarat, India, earlier this month. The Trump administration has defended the lifting of restrictions on Russia and Iran as necessary to prevent further spikes in energy prices that could ripple through the global economy and hit American consumers.

item.title: Leonid Radvinsky, 43, Dies; Built the Adult-Entertainment Giant OnlyFans
item.description: By leveraging social media and the influencer economy, he turned his website into a byword for online pornography in the 21st century.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Asia Is Getting Crushed Between Oil Prices and the Dollar
item.description: From India to Southeast Asia to South Korea, currencies are crumbling as governments race to secure fuel that is priced in American money.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli, Choe Sang-Hun, Sui-Lee Wee and Jason Gutierrez
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Shammi Mehra/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: People lining up to refuel at a gas station in Ahmedabad in India on Monday.

item.title: How Kharg Island May Change the Trajectory of the Iran War
item.description: Kharg Island exports 90 percent of Iran’s crude oil. It has also become a potential U.S. target. Peter Eavis, our Business reporter, examines how the small island in the Persian Gulf has become a strategic target with significant risks.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis, Gilad Thaler, Edward Vega, Lauren Pruitt and Joey Sendaydiego
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: The Oil Price Shock Could Make Italian Ice More Expensive
item.description: Higher energy prices caused by the war with Iran could affect more than just prices at the pump for American consumers.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ondrey and Mia Lawson, who own Phrostbite Italian Ice in Houston, are altering their catering schedule and redrawing delivery routes to save money on fuel.

item.title: Supreme Court Sides With Internet Provider in Copyright Fight Over Pirated Music
item.description: Leading music labels sued Cox Communications for failing to terminate accounts of subscribers flagged for distributing copyrighted music.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images
item.media:description: At issue for the justices was whether providers like Cox could be held legally responsible if they knew that customers were pirating music but did not take steps to terminate their internet access.

item.title: As Gas Prices Spike, California Is Hit Hardest
item.description: A gallon has climbed to nearly $6 in the state, about $2 above the national average.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Kurtis Lee
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mike Blake/Reuters
item.media:description: A gas station in Los Angeles this month. Critics of oil companies that operate in California say refiners have taken advantage of crises like the war with Iran to raise prices.

item.title: What Sora’s End Says About OpenAI’s Strategy
item.description: The artificial intelligence start-up has pulled the plug on the video-making tool as it rethinks spending ahead of a possible blockbuster I.P.O.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon for The New York Times
item.media:description: Led by Sam Altman, OpenAI appears set to embark on a round of financial belt-tightening.

item.title: What Happened After a U.S. Warship Hit an Iranian Mine in 1988
item.description: President Trump has suggested providing escorts for stranded tankers. A mine strike on a U.S. frigate in the Persian Gulf shows the perils of doing so.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: The U.S. frigate Samuel B. Roberts a day after an Iranian mine damaged it in April 1988.

item.title: Can Flights Get Any Worse? Travelers Deal With TSA Lines, High Ticket Prices and Anxiety.
item.description: Travelers are waiting hours at security checkpoints, paying top dollar for tickets and worrying about safety after a deadly crash at LaGuardia.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung and Ceylan Yeğinsu
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: A shortage of Transportation Security Administration officers and the aftermath of a plane crash combined to cause long delays at LaGuardia Airport, in New York, on Tuesday.

item.title: The Brigade System Helps Restaurants Succeed. Does It Also Lead to Abuse?
item.description: Allegations against Noma’s chef have spurred debate over whether a 19th-century model for organizing kitchen staffs breeds physical and psychic violence.
item.dc:creator: Pete Wells

item.title: How Daily Money Managers Help Busy People With Their Finances
item.description: Daily money managers generally help older adults and the wealthy, but busy professionals are increasingly using their services when they get behind.
item.dc:creator: Paulette Perhach
item.media:credit: David Degner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tulsi Vadodaria is a daily money manager who helps clients organize their finances, deal with overdue bills and get expired checks reissued.

item.title: Senate Housing Bill Sparks Debate About Who Gets to Own Single-Family Homes
item.description: Within the Senate’s housing bill lie the terms of an unusual debate: Who gets to own — and live in — single-family homes?
item.dc:creator: Conor Dougherty and Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: A rental home community being built in Simpsonville, S.C.

item.title: What Soaring Fuel Costs Mean for Your Air Travel
item.description: The price of jet fuel has almost doubled since the start of the war. Our reporter Niraj Chokshi, who covers aviation, describes what that will mean for flights.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi, Léo Hamelin, Stephanie Swart, Rebecca Suner and Lauren Pruitt

item.title: Cheap Drones Remain Wild Card in Iran war
item.description: Stopping Iran’s production of drones is critical to opening the Strait of Hormuz and halting its attacks on Gulf nations. But can it be done?
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kulish
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A missile and a Shahed drone on display during a military parade in Tehran in 2025.

item.title: Write It Up! How Fast Do Our Journalists Type?
item.description: When it comes to breaking news, it’s a race not only to get the story, but to record it. We tracked down some of the speediest fingers in our newsroom.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Oil Prices Fall as Trump Shows Eagerness to Talk to Iran
item.description: Investors reacted to signals of possible de-escalation of the war in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times

item.title: Iran Says ‘Non-Hostile’ Ships Can Sail Through the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Ships with no ties to Israel or the United States would be allowed to pass, the government said, but it was unclear if any vessels would try.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Cargo ships in the Gulf, near the Strait of Hormuz, seen from the United Arab Emirates earlier this month.

item.title: New York Times Accuses Pentagon of Defying Court Order
item.description: The company said in a legal filing that the department sought to fashion an “end run” when it issued revised media rules on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times accused the Pentagon of adopting a fresh round of media restrictions in retaliation for an adverse ruling from a federal judge last week.

item.title: Delta Air Lines Says It Will Suspend Special Services for Congress Members
item.description: Airport escorts and “red coat” assistance for lawmakers will be suspended, the airline said on Tuesday, citing the extended partial government shutdown.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Stefani Reynolds/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Delta Air Lines said that it would suspend special services for members of Congress, amid a stalemate over funding for the Department of Homeland Security.

item.title: New Limits on Investors and a Debt Downgrade Add to Private Credit Woes
item.description: Investors are concerned about the health of the industry, which ballooned over the past decade.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Farrell
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Michael Arougheti, co-founder and chief executive of Ares Management, an investment firm that said Tuesday that 11.6 percent of investors in one of its funds had sought to redeem their shares.

item.title: High Oil and Gas Prices Could Outlast Trump’s War With Iran
item.description: While the president has promised rapid relief, Americans could feel the financial sting of the conflict for some time after it ends.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Even when the conflict ends, it may be weeks or months before American families and businesses see a true break in spiraling energy costs.

item.title: Epic Games Lays Off Over 1,000 Employees, Citing Fortnite Slump
item.description: The cut represents about 20 percent of the video game company’s work force, a spokeswoman said.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Epic Games booth at the GDC Game Developers Conference in San Francisco in 2019. The company cited a “downturn in Fortnite engagement” starting last year when it announced layoffs on Tuesday.

item.title: Russian Oil Shipment Puts Focus on Kremlin Spy Outpost in Cuba
item.description: Moscow may be challenging President Trump’s effort to choke Cuba’s economy. China also has suspected listening posts on the island.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Norlys Perez/Reuters
item.media:description: Mr. Trump has spoken of using his economic leverage to mount a “friendly takeover” of Cuba and is pressing for the ouster of its president, Miguel Díaz-Canel.

item.title: Covid Relief Loans Are Haunting Small Businesses
item.description: The Small Business Administration lent $378 billion to keep businesses afloat. Getting paid back is proving difficult.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Anna Ottum for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chris Towns, a blueberry farmer in Alma, Ga., took out hundreds of thousands in federal loans during the pandemic.

item.title: War in Iran Is Disrupting Air Travel, Hitting Middle East Hardest
item.description: Some airlines and travelers, especially in the Middle East and elsewhere in Asia, are bearing the brunt of the pain, while others are doing better.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi, Pragati K.B. and Aaron Krolik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Hassan Ammar/Associated Press
item.media:description: A plane taking off from Beirut-Rafik Hariri International Airport in Lebanon last week, as smoke rose from the site of an Israeli airstrike.
